ratings.rationale: The four emotional states are familiar and the clues point cleanly to each attitude with little thematic overlap.
The categories are recognizable historical shifts, but solving depends on some German and European history knowledge across several eras.
The philosophies are common enough and the clue language is direct, though nihilism and pragmatism can briefly blur at first glance.
Mapping musical-aesthetic movements from poetic image-heavy clues requires genre knowledge and tolerating several abstract descriptions.
The nature images consistently mirror emotional or situational states, making the intended groupings fairly legible despite mild metaphor.
The food-culture groupings are concrete, but some clues rely on German-speaking cultural familiarity rather than pure lexical signals.
The political categories are distinct and coherent, though collaboration and propaganda can create a few authoritarian-regime false trails.
These language registers and varieties are highly familiar and the clue wording usually names the category almost outright.
Architectural styles are separable only if the solver knows technical markers and can ignore some historically noisy details.
All four climate-impact categories are topical and the examples are vivid, concrete, and easy to sort.
The theatrical forms are conceptually neat, but distinguishing satire from comedy and recalling epic theater signals adds some pressure.
The epistemic categories are standard and the clues are mostly explicit, with only intuition and tradition posing minor ambiguity.
Weather phenomena are familiar, but Föhn in particular is region-specific and the atmospheric clues can overlap before the set clicks.
These professions are everyday and each set of actions strongly anchors one job with almost no abstraction.
The visual style markers are concrete and distinctive, even if Jugendstil versus Brutalismus still asks for some architecture vocabulary.
The child-psychology concepts are coherent but somewhat abstract, and animism, magic, and allmacht can initially feel adjacent.
The social attitudes in sport are intuitive and the examples are plain behavioral cues rather than tricky wordplay.
Traditional crafts are less familiar and several clues depend on specialized process vocabulary that many solvers will not know.
Nautical operations use dense seamanship terminology, making this one notably harder unless the solver already knows sailing language.
The school formats are common knowledge and the classroom scenarios are straightforwardly diagnostic.
Dog-behavior categories are fairly intuitive, though dominance, fear, and play signals can momentarily cross for non-experts.
Bridge types are structurally distinct once recognized, but solving requires some engineering vocabulary and image-building from the clues.
Body-language groupings are accessible, but several cues are culturally fuzzy or pseudo-diagnostic enough to invite false grouping.
The travel mindsets are broad familiar themes, and the clue sets separate cleanly once the emotional framing is noticed.
